entries.what_they_read: I saw 'Trade player tokens that move with real stats' up top, then scanned down to the $100 offer and the three-step breakdown — especially that LeBron example with the $5.59 fair value versus $4.20 market price.
entries.their_interpretation: Looks like a paper-trading sandbox where someone's built a model that prices NBA players off box scores, and the game is spotting where the crowd got it wrong before they figure it out — basically value investing but for basketball stats.
entries.their_decision: I'd click 'Claim $100' and poke around the player cards.
entries.their_rationale: No signup, no seed phrase, no real money — the friction is basically zero and the concept maps cleanly onto something I already do with assets, so the cost of being wrong here is just a few minutes. The LeBron gap example is the kind of mispricing I actually look for, just in a different market.
confusion_notes: 'Devnet' — I know this from Solana context but most people landing here won't; it's doing quiet work that could easily be missed
'AWAITING TIP-OFF' banner — not sure if that means the app is broken right now or just waiting for tonight's games to start
'Buying raises the price for the next person' — this sounds like a bonding curve mechanic but they never say that; I'd want to know the actual pricing formula before I trust the 'fair value' number
first_impression_elements: The LeBron $5.59 vs $4.20 gap example — that's the clearest thing on the page, made the concept click immediately
'Claim $100 →' button — big, early, hard to miss
'No account yet · $100 · no real money · no seed phrase' subline under the button — that's the line that lowered my guard
ignored_elements: The 'Methodology' link in the footer — I noticed it exists but didn't click; I'll want it later if the fair-value math seems off
'↓Terms explained' — skipped it, the page already told me it's fake money so I figured the terms were just boilerplate
The 'About' and 'Invite' nav links — background noise at this stage

checkpoint.q1_what_am_i_buying: Far as I can tell, it's a fake token tied to a real player — not ownership of anything, not a real asset. It's a position in a pretend market that tracks whether the crowd is pricing that player right against what his stats say he's worth.
checkpoint.q2_what_moves_price: Two things, the way I read it: the stats feed moves the fair-value number daily, and then other people buying or selling moves the market price. If everybody panics and dumps a guy, market price drops even if his stats are still solid — that's where I'd be looking to step in.
checkpoint.q3_hold_week: Honestly I'm not totally sure. The fair-value price updates with real stats every day, so I'd expect it to drift as he plays games. Whether the market price follows on its own or only moves when people actually trade — the app didn't spell that out clearly to me. I'd be watching the gap, not just sitting blind.
checkpoint.q4_fair_vs_market: Fair value is what the computer says he's worth based on his actual stats. Market price is what people are actually paying — emotion, hype, panic, all of it baked in. The gap between those two numbers is the whole game. I want to buy when market is below fair value and the crowd hasn't noticed yet.

first_impression_elements: The orange 'real stats.' text in the hero headline — high contrast, pulls the eye immediately
Yellow CTA button 'Claim $100' — bold, warm against the dark grid background, hard to miss
The grid-lined dark background giving it a trading-terminal aesthetic
ignored_elements: The 'DEVNET · PRACTICE MODE' nav items top right — felt like system status noise
The 'NEW HERE? 10-SECOND VERSION.' section below the fold — didn't pull me down
data.visual_notes: Dark grid background is a smart choice for a trading product — it signals precision and data without screaming crypto-bro. Typography hierarchy is clean: the headline weight contrast and orange accent do real work. The micro-copy beneath the CTA ('no real money · no seed phrase') is quietly reassuring — someone thought about friction. The page doesn't oversell itself, which I respect.
